sections.title: Overview
sections.content: This guideline addresses ergonomic issues arising from camp rooms, dining, office pods, and other living/working crossover spaces on site.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Neck, back, shoulder, or sleep-related complaints linked to room or office setup
- Symptoms worsened by laptop use, poor chairs, narrow beds, or repeated awkward reaching
- Recovery limited by poor rest or poor non-work environment

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Camp ergonomics can affect both recovery and next-shift function
- Rest and sleep environments are legitimate contributors to musculoskeletal load
- Simple changes often outperform expensive but impractical ideal setups

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Ask which parts of the camp environment make symptoms worse and what can be changed immediately
- Encourage posture variation, setup changes, and movement breaks
- Escalate if symptoms clearly reflect something more serious than ergonomic aggravation

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Review seating, screens, bed setup, storage reach, and repetitive non-work tasks such as laundry or shared amenities
- Recommend practical camp-based changes that the worker can actually maintain
- Pair ergonomic advice with active rehab rather than relying on setup changes alone

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Good camp ergonomics supports recovery outside work hours, which may improve work performance indirectly
